sentences.text: Lily Lipman, L-I-L-Y L-I-P-M-A-N.
My name at birth was [?
Berzowsky. ?] But when my parents came to South Africa, it was easier to use the word-- surname Berzow, B-E-R-Z-O-W.
But I still have a cousin whose name is [?
Berzowsky, ?] who lives in Poland.
My mother called me Lilenka.
L-I-L-E-N-K-A.
I'm not sure.
On the naturalization certificate, it's 14/7/23.
I celebrate the 26th of July.
And I think I was born in '24 or '25.
But my parents had to send me to school when I arrived in South Africa.
So they pushed me into school and gave me an arbitrary date of birth.
I think I'm 70, 71.
But not 73, as it would be if I was born in '23.
It was Poland, in Vilna.
But now Vilna is in Lithuania.
But when I was born, it was still part of Poland.
Nothing.
Absolutely nothing.
My first memories of anything was when we arrived in South Africa.
I think that-- that we lived with my grandparents, my father's parents, because they were Polish.
My mother was Lithuanian.
And we lived in their house.
And we came to South Africa-- or my father came to South Africa because a cousin of his, resident in South Africa, came to visit the family.
And my father had just been to the post office.
And he was in the queue.
And he was told by a Pole, you should get back to the back of the queue.
And just at that time, my father's cousin was in Vilna.
And my father related that story.
And he said, well, it's time for you to get out.
And I'll see if I can get you a-- I don't know.
There was some-- some way that one could get people to come to South Africa.
And he went back to-- to Johannesburg.
And about six months later, my father announced that he had got the right papers and he was going to Johannesburg, and as soon as he could, he would bring the family to Johannesburg.
He left in 1927.
And in 1929, we arrived in South Africa, in Johannesburg.
My mother and my sister.
And my first real memory is Johannesburg.
And in fact, Johannesburg, where my mother pushed me into school because she had to start working.
And I went to [?
Dorfintine ?] School, which was a school where all the immigrant children were going.
And we had an Afrikaans teacher who used to say, [AFRIKAANS] which means stand up, donkeys, sit down, donkeys.
I don't think she really understood the traumas that the immigrants were going through.
Three or four.
But my mother pushed me into school, and said that I was six.
That was the starting age of the children.
But I definitely wasn't six.
I was probably four or five.

What I do remember, which I really wasn't very happy about, was that my mother and father, particularly my mother, loved to ingratiate herself with the teachers by bringing them wine on Pesach-- and none of them were Jewish-- and matzoh.
And when they came to the school, I would run away and pretend that they weren't my parents-- weren't my mother.
And I think I envied the girls who were, as I called, [NON-ENGLISH], which means that they were born in South Africa and they had parents who could talk English.
My parents, of course, spoke with an accent, and it was embarrassing for me.
My parents, they spoke in Russian, because their-- their upbringing was-- they went to Russian schools, because Poland and Lithuania was then under Russia.
And they-- our home language was Russian.
And in fact, I spoke a fluent Russian until I went to high school.
And then they introduced Yiddish into the home, and I learned Yiddish.
And when I went to work in Germany with the displaced persons, I was the only English-speaking person who could speak Russian.
And a lot of the displaced persons were speaking in Russian then because they had-- they had often been in Siberia, and had come back to Germany to look-- and in Poland to look for their relatives.
And there weren't any.
And they came through to Germany, to the displaced persons camps.
I wanted to become a doctor.
[LAUGHS] And my mother and father were devastated because that would mean that I'd be an old maid.
So they took me to [LAUGHS] I think one of the few women doctors that were practicing at the time.
And they said to her, just tell her what you think about being a doctor.
And she said, if you ever want to get married, don't do medicine.
And so I didn't do medicine.
[LAUGHTER] I did social work instead.
And I'm very, very much happier that I did social work because it gave me the opportunity to travel and to really be with people.
At Wits-- Witwatersrand.
But [INAUDIBLE] that, I pushed both my sons into medicine.
So there must have been something--
--about my desire to do medicine that came through in my family.
Nothing.
Nothing at all.
My parents never spoke about it.
They never spoke about their families in Europe.
Whether it was to protect us or whether they weren't interested, I'm not sure.
But my first knowledge of what was going on in the war was when I actually arrived in Munich in 1946.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
I was out to have a good time and to get married.
Well, I studied social work.
And I think I was the second group-- I was in the second group of students who qualified.
And the South African Jewish War Appeal were looking for graduates to send to Germany.
And somebody said to me, wouldn't you like to go overseas?
And I said, yes, I'd love to go overseas.
And I went off to the Jewish War Appeal.
And they asked me my age.
I said I was 25, which I wasn't, and they didn't query it.
I must've been 23 or 22 at the time.
It was 1946.
If I was born in 1925, I was 23.
And if I was born in 1924, I was 24.
And they didn't ask for birth certificates, and I didn't have one anyway.
And there were so few social workers who had graduated that they grabbed me.
And-- and so I went via London to Paris, and from Paris to Munich.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my-- I was under UNRRA.

But actually, because it was the South African Jewish War Appeal that sent me, I was attached to AJDC, which was the American Joint Distribution Committee, which was working with the Jewish displaced persons in Germany and elsewhere.
UNRRA was the United Nations Relief Rehabilitation Administration, which was at the head of all the private organizations like AJDC, and then the Jewish Agency, which came from Palestine.
We had quite a lot of Jewish Agency personnel at the camps that our workers looked at.
My immediate boss was the UNRRA director.
But AJDC was, in a way, a boss as well, because AJDC were the ones-- it was the organization that sent me where I was needed.
But in every camp, there was an UNRRA, which became the-- the directors to which I had to report.
Yes.
Yes, very much so.
The AJDC person was Seal.
And Seal was busy with my uncle.
And the person in charge of UNRRA was a Miss Gertrude Richman, which was-- who was a most outstanding person.
And although she wasn't Jewish, she had a tremendous empathy for the displaced persons, and did a tremendous amount of good work.
When I arrived in Paris with another South African, I was really just keen to look around Paris and-- and get to know what the provisions were like.
But my uncle, who had got a-- received a telegram from my mother-- no.
My uncle sent a telegram to my mother to say that he had survived it.
It was my mother's youngest brother.
And when he heard from my mother, who wired him back that I was coming to work with UNRRA and AJDC, he immediately phoned through to AJDC in Paris and insisted that I come there right away.
And so the other South Africans who were in Paris had a good time, and I had to go off to meet my uncle.
And he met me in Munich.
Zally, Z--
Well, I spelled it S-A-L-L-Y.
But I take it must have been Z-A-L-L-Y.
And his surname was Zeed, which was my mother's maiden name.
And Zally took me to the house where he was living.
And that house a-- Bavarian villa in Munich, which had about 15 rooms.
And in each room was a survivor family.
And he took me and introduced me to every member of the family that had survived.
Each one was in one room.
And they were friends as well.
And each one grabbed me and started telling me their horror stories, and for the first time that I had actually heard about the Holocaust.
I don't think the word Holocaust was then a word.
But it was the first time that I heard any of these horror stories.
And when I reported to the AJDC director, Seal, I said to her, look, I don't want to stay.
I want to go home.
I don't want to hear any more of these stories.
And she became very angry with me.
And she said, do you think that we spent our money to bring you to have a good time?
Just pull yourself together.
Don't listen to the stories here, but don't listen, because if you think you're going home, you're not.
And so I suddenly, through some kind of shock treatment, realized that I was here to work in Munich.
I was there to work and not to enjoy myself.
Well, I had to report to Seal first.
And after a few days-- I stayed with my uncle at this villa, listening to these stories.
And I had to report to Seal.
And she decided that the best for me would be to go to the children's center, because I was very young.
And so she sent me off to Rosenheim, which was a very large center.
It was an old army-- old German barracks.
And it had at the time 3,000 children.
And they were arranged in-- like kibbutz groups.
There were the kibbutz leader and probably about 30 or 40 children that were under him or her.
And they lived in dormitories.
And there were a few adults.
But when I first arrived, it was mainly just children.
And it's all very interesting, the story of these children.
And I didn't realize this until much later.
The Jewish Agency-- because Israel was still Palestine, the Jewish Agency had sent their personnel to the camps to work with the children.

But before they sent them to the camps, they sent a lot of them to Poland and to Lithuania, where families had come-- had either survived and gone back to Poland-- but most of them that were in our camps were not so much survivors of the camps, but-- but people who had been driven by the-- the Poles out of Poland into Russia at the beginning of the war.
And fortunately, the Russians sent them to Siberia, because the Russians were not very pro-Jews and they thought that they might become spies.
So they sent them to Siberia.
And most of these were the survivors of the Siberian camps.
And after the war, they came back to their old villages.
And of course, there was no one left.
And so they either went to Germany as families-- but mostly the Jewish Agency couldn't take them as families.
So they came and took the children, with a promise to the parents that as soon as they had organized these children in the displaced persons camps, they would come back for the parents.
And so the parents allowed their children to be separated from them.
And these were the children that we had at the Rosenheim camp.
Unfortunately, soon after, the Polish government closed their borders so that the Jewish Agency could not take the parents out.
And they were left to work in the camps with the children.
My first role was to settle the children in the camps, and then to open nursery schools for the very young, schools for the school-going children, ORT workshops for the adults.

To teach the people a trade-- how to-- tailoring, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] carpentry, shoemaking-- the kind of skills or the kind of crafts that one wasn't used to having in the-- you know, in the ghettos-- or rather, in Siberia.
I'm not sure.

But those were the workshops that ORT in America or the AJDC opened for these people-- carpentry workshops, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shoe-- shoe, tanneries, shoemaking, sewing-- a lot of sewing was done by the women-- and leatherwork.
I'm not sure.
But we have ORT in South Africa too.
And that also deals with teaching-- vocational guidance.
I associate ORT with vocational guidance.
At that stage, [INAUDIBLE].
The-- the impact came when I first arrived in Munich with my uncle.

That was a-- a tremendous trauma and impact because those people had been survivors of the camps, whereas the children in the displaced person camps were not survivors in the sense that they were in concentration camps.
They had spent most of their lives in Siberia, and so they weren't so traumatized.
And the-- the madrichim that were in charge of them were often from-- from Palestine.
And they were very sympathetic and very good with them.
And they were mainly late teenagers or early-- in the early 20's and 30's, and they had a very good relationship.
And they acted, I would say, as jolly good parent substitutes.
I didn't get the feeling, although it's a long time ago-- but I didn't think I had the feeling as a social worker that these children were missing their parents.
They seemed to be very happy with their madrichim.
Well, they varied.
We had some very young children, nursery school, from about four to five.
And then there were a lot of schoolchildren, 6 to 12 and 14, 15.
And then there were some adolescents.
And then there were the adults who actually were able to come out either with families-- those were very few.
Mostly adults who came on their own.
They had enormous rooms-- army barracks.
They-- they lived in dormitories, with the-- the madrichim sleeping with them in dormitories-- 20, sometimes 25 to a large dormitory on stretchers with covers.
I don't think I told you that I graduated as a social worker.
Did I say that?
I think I did, yes-- and that when I graduated as a social worker, I worked in play centers.
So my experience in play centers actually was very useful for me in organizing nursery schools, and-- and play centers, and schools.
What were the actual needs of the children, would you say-- the-- the main needs that they had?
I think that they needed the stimulation of a nursery school environment, and the children who were school-going age had to start learning.
The supplies for the schoolchildren came from AJDC.
And Munich and Rosenheim were in the American zone.
And we had tremendous, tremendous, tremendous assistance from the American Army.
They were very sympathetic, very helpful.
And in fact, one of those things that I thought would be very useful for them was to open a gymnasium for the school-going children.
And I became friendly with some of the GIs.
And the GIs, of course, weren't allowed at that stage to fraternize with the-- with the German Frauleins.
It was against-- it was against the law, the American law.
So any young girl was very much in demand.
And I was young at the time, one of the youngest.
In fact, I was the youngest at-- in-- in UNRRA and in AJDC.
And I was able to get almost anything I wanted because I was a female and-- and they were interested in-- in taking girls out.
So I managed to get a fully equipped gymnasium from the American Army.
And they actually transported all the equipment to Rosenheim Camp.
And that was a tremendous, tremendous boon for the young children.
I've never seen anything like it.
Do you remember some of the GIs?
No, except they were GIs and probably 23, 24.
They were in the American Army of Occupation because Germany was then occupied.
Well, I must say that I didn't come from a religious home, so my knowledge of the Jewish festivals was limited.
But one of the-- one of the great moments of my life in that camp was the first Pesach Seder that took place at Rosenheim after the war.
It was 194-- 47, because I arrived in-- in Munich in July of '46.
So in April, March-April '47, it was the first time that these children were able to have a Pesach Seder.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the person who was taking the Seder, the person who was invited by the AJDC and the Jewish Agency, was a Palestinian man who was rather important in Palestine at the time by the name of Nachum Levine.
I remember his name.
I quite clearly remember his face.
He was, I would say, a man in his 50's.
To me, that was ancient.
And he came.
And he ran the service.
But the Jewish Agency personnel on our staff, on the UNRRA staff-- because just as I was under UNRRA, so were the Jewish Agency personnel under UNRRA.
And the two personnel from the Jewish Agency, Abram Zur and [INAUDIBLE] can't think of her surname-- did all the necessaries.
And they organized the-- the large tables and all the food that was necessary.
And that, the AJDC sent from America.
And not being very familiar with what had to be done, I just followed them and asked them, for instance, what must I do?
What must I do?
And just followed their instructions.
And it was an enormously exciting event because it-- it-- it took place in a large communal hall with, I would say, 2,000 to 3,000 people.
And everyone was very excited.
And we borrowed microphones from the American Army.
And the-- the Seder took place.
The-- it took about two or three hours to go through the Seder.
And they had matzoh and everything.
It was really exciting.
And I have pictures, which perhaps we could show later.
It was very special because my parents really didn't go in for the ceremonies, and I really didn't know very much about it.
